unit: Unit 14: Linear Algebra Foundations for Systems
title: Linear Algebra Foundations for Systems
subtitle: Vectors, Transformations, Determinants, and Eigentheory
cheatSubtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Span, Linear Maps, Determinants, and Eigenvalues
description: The geometric linear algebra core needed before systems of differential equations: linear combinations and span, linear transformations as matrices, the determinant as a signed area-scaling factor, change of basis and similarity, and eigentheory. The unit closes by feeding eigenvalues and eigenvectors straight into the linear system x' = Ax, where each eigenpair becomes an exponential mode and the whole machinery of Unit 15 is set up.

atAGlance.title: Unit 14 at a Glance: Linear Algebra for Systems
atAGlance.summary: Before coupled differential equations can be solved, the language of linear algebra has to be in place. A matrix is read geometrically: its columns say where the basis vectors land, its determinant says how it scales area and whether it flips orientation, and its eigenvectors mark the special directions it merely stretches. The payoff is the eigenvalue method --- guessing $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ turns the system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into the algebraic eigenvalue problem $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$.
bullets: \textbf{Vectors and transformations} --- linear combinations, span, basis, and the matrix whose \emph{columns are the images of the basis vectors}.
\textbf{The determinant} --- the signed area-scaling factor $ad-bc$; zero means collapse, negative means an orientation flip.
\textbf{Change of basis} --- the similarity transform $P^{-1}AP$ rewrites the same map in new coordinates; an eigenbasis makes it diagonal.
\textbf{Eigentheory} --- $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$, the characteristic equation $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$, and the trace/determinant shortcut for $2\times 2$.
\textbf{Eigenvalues through ODEs} --- $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ decouples $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into scalar modes, the gateway to Unit 15.

modules.title: Vectors, Span, and Linear Transformations
modules.topics: Linear combinations, span, and basis; linear transformations and the matrices that encode them; columns as images of the basis vectors; composition as matrix product.

formulas.title: Linearity Condition
formulas.body: T(a\mathbf{u} + b\mathbf{v}) = a\,T(\mathbf{u}) + b\,T(\mathbf{v}) \quad \text{for all scalars } a,b \text{ and vectors } \mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}.

formulas.title: The Matrix of a Transformation
formulas.body: A = \big[\, T(\mathbf{e}_1) \mid T(\mathbf{e}_2) \mid \cdots \,\big], \qquad T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x}, \qquad (T_2 \circ T_1) \leftrightarrow A_2 A_1.

methodology.title: Method --- Build the Matrix of a Linear Map
steps: Apply $T$ to each standard basis vector $\mathbf{e}_j$.
Assemble the columns into the matrix $A$.
Read off the action as $T(\mathbf{x}) = A\mathbf{x}$ and verify on a test vector.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Apply a Matrix to a Vector
examples.setup: Let $A$ have rows $(1,2)$ and $(3,4)$. Compute the image of $\mathbf{x}=(1,1)$ by dotting each row with $\mathbf{x}$.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: A linear transformation is \textcolor{accentorange}{completely determined by where it sends the basis}. The columns of $A$ are exactly the images $T(\mathbf{e}_j)$, and composing two maps corresponds to \emph{multiplying} their matrices (right-to-left).

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: A map with a nonzero shift, $T(\mathbf{u}) = \mathbf{u} + \mathbf{c}$ with $\mathbf{c}\neq\mathbf{0}$, is \textcolor{warnred}{affine, not linear} --- it moves the origin. Linearity \emph{requires} $T(\mathbf{0})=\mathbf{0}$. Also, matrix multiplication is not commutative: $A_2A_1\neq A_1A_2$ in general, so composition order matters.

quiz.prompt: What is a linear combination of vectors $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{w}$?
What is the span of a set of vectors?
quiz.answer: The set of all linear combinations of those vectors.

quiz.prompt: What two conditions make a set a basis?
quiz.answer: It must be linearly independent and span the space.

quiz.prompt: Which identity must a linear transformation satisfy?
What do the columns of a transformation matrix represent?
quiz.answer: The images of the standard basis vectors under the transformation.

quiz.prompt: How is the composition of two linear maps represented?
quiz.answer: By the product of their matrices, applied right to left.

modules.title: The Determinant and Change of Basis
modules.topics: The determinant as a signed area-scaling factor; the formula $ad-bc$; what zero and negative determinants mean; change-of-basis matrices and the similarity transform $P^{-1}AP$.

formulas.title: The $2\times 2$ Determinant
formulas.body: \det\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} = ad - bc \;=\; \text{signed factor by which the map scales area.}

formulas.title: Reading the Determinant Geometrically
formulas.body: \lvert\det A\rvert = \text{area scaling}, \quad \det A = 0 \Rightarrow \text{collapse (dependent columns)}, \quad \det A < 0 \Rightarrow \text{orientation reversed.}

formulas.title: Change of Basis and Similarity
formulas.body: B = P^{-1} A P, \qquad \text{columns of } P = \text{new basis vectors in the old coordinates}; \quad \text{eigenbasis} \Rightarrow B \text{ diagonal.}

methodology.title: Method --- Re-express a Map in a New Basis
steps: Place the new basis vectors (written in old coordinates) as the columns of $P$.
Form the similarity transform $B = P^{-1} A P$.
If the columns of $P$ are eigenvectors of $A$, then $B$ is diagonal with the eigenvalues on the diagonal.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Determinant and Its Meaning
examples.setup: Compute the determinant of $A$ with rows $(3,1)$ and $(2,4)$, then interpret it geometrically.
steps: \lvert\det A\rvert = 10 \Rightarrow \text{areas are scaled by a factor of } 10

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: The determinant packages two facts in one number: its \textcolor{accentorange}{magnitude is the area-scaling factor} and its \textcolor{accentorange}{sign records orientation}. Because a change of basis only relabels coordinates, \emph{similar} matrices $P^{-1}AP$ share the same determinant, trace, and eigenvalues.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: Do not confuse a \textcolor{warnred}{negative} determinant with a \textcolor{warnred}{zero} determinant. Negative still means invertible --- the plane is merely flipped. Only $\det A = 0$ collapses a dimension and makes $A$ singular (columns linearly dependent).

quiz.prompt: Geometrically, what does a $2\times 2$ determinant measure?
quiz.answer: The factor by which the transformation scales areas.

quiz.prompt: For rows $(a,b)$ and $(c,d)$, what is the determinant?
Compute the determinant of rows $(3,1)$ and $(2,4)$.
What does a determinant of $0$ tell you?
quiz.answer: It collapses space to a lower dimension; the columns are linearly dependent.

quiz.prompt: What does a negative determinant indicate?
quiz.answer: The transformation reverses orientation (it flips the plane).

quiz.prompt: In a change-of-basis matrix, what do the columns contain?
quiz.answer: The new basis vectors written in the old coordinates.

quiz.prompt: How is a transformation $A$ expressed in a new basis $P$?
Why is an eigenbasis a useful change of basis?
quiz.answer: It diagonalizes the matrix, so the action is just scaling each axis.

modules.title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
modules.topics: The eigenvalue equation $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$; the characteristic equation $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$; trace as the sum and determinant as the product of eigenvalues; the trace/determinant shortcut for $2\times 2$ matrices.

formulas.title: The Eigenvalue Equation
formulas.body: A\mathbf{v} = \lambda\mathbf{v}, \quad \mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}: \quad A \text{ only \emph{stretches} } \mathbf{v} \text{ along its own line, by the factor } \lambda.

formulas.title: Trace--Determinant Shortcut
formulas.body: \lambda = m \pm \sqrt{m^2 - p}, \quad m = \tfrac{1}{2}\operatorname{tr}A,\ \ p = \det A; \qquad \operatorname{tr}A = \lambda_1+\lambda_2,\ \ \det A = \lambda_1\lambda_2.

methodology.title: Method --- Find Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
steps: Set $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$ and solve for $\lambda$ (for $2\times 2$, use $\lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda+\det A=0$).
For each $\lambda$, solve $(A-\lambda I)\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ for the eigenvector $\mathbf{v}$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors of a Symmetric Matrix
examples.setup: For $A$ with rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$, use $\operatorname{tr}A=4$ and $\det A=3$, then find an eigenvector for each eigenvalue.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: Eigenvectors are the \textcolor{accentorange}{special directions a matrix leaves invariant} --- it only scales them, by the eigenvalue. In the eigenbasis the matrix becomes diagonal, which is exactly what makes powers, exponentials, and differential equations easy.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: The characteristic equation is $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$, \textcolor{warnred}{not} $\det A - \lambda = 0$, and the $2\times 2$ form carries a \emph{minus} sign on the trace: $\lambda^2-(\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda+\det A$. Handy check: for diagonal or triangular matrices the eigenvalues sit on the diagonal --- the off-diagonal entries do not change them.

quiz.prompt: What equation defines an eigenvector $\mathbf{v}$ with eigenvalue $\lambda$?
Geometrically, what is special about an eigenvector?
quiz.answer: It stays on its own line through the origin, only stretched or compressed.

quiz.prompt: The eigenvalues of $A$ solve which equation?
Eigenvalues of the triangular matrix rows $(2,7)$ and $(0,4)$?
For a $2\times 2$ matrix, the eigenvalues are roots of which quadratic?
In $\lambda = m \pm \sqrt{m^2-p}$, what are $m$ and $p$?
Eigenvalues of rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$ (trace $4$, det $3$)?
The sum of the eigenvalues equals what?

modules.title: Eigenvalues Through Differential Equations
modules.topics: The trial solution $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ that reduces $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ to the eigenvalue problem; one exponential mode per eigenpair; the general solution by superposition; growth, decay, and oscillation from the sign and type of the eigenvalues.

formulas.title: The Eigen-Ansatz
formulas.body: \text{Try } \mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}: \quad \mathbf{x}'=\lambda e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} = A e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v} \ \Rightarrow\ A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}.

formulas.title: General Solution (Distinct Eigenvalues)
formulas.body: \mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\mathbf{v}_2 \quad (\text{superposition of the eigen-modes}).

methodology.title: Method --- Solve $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ by Eigenvalues
steps: Find the eigenvalues of $A$ from $\det(A-\lambda I)=0$.
Find an eigenvector $\mathbf{v}_i$ for each eigenvalue $\lambda_i$.
Superpose: $\mathbf{x}=\sum_i c_i e^{\lambda_i t}\mathbf{v}_i$, then fix the $c_i$ from the initial conditions.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Solve a Coupled System
examples.setup: Solve $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A$ with rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$, reusing the eigenpairs $\lambda_1=3,\ \mathbf{v}_1=(1,1)$ and $\lambda_2=1,\ \mathbf{v}_2=(1,-1)$ from 14.3.
steps: \text{Each eigenpair gives a mode: } e^{3t}(1,1) \text{ and } e^{t}(1,-1)
\text{Both } \lambda>0 \Rightarrow \text{every trajectory grows; the origin is an unstable node.}

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: The eigenvalue method \textcolor{accentorange}{decouples a coupled linear system into independent scalar modes}. Along each eigenvector $A$ acts as multiplication by $\lambda$, so $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ collapses to $x'=\lambda x$ with solution $e^{\lambda t}$ --- the foundation of every technique in Unit 15.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: In the superposition each exponential must ride its \textcolor{warnred}{own} eigenvector: $c_1 e^{\lambda_1 t}\mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{\lambda_2 t}\mathbf{v}_2$, never a crossed pairing. And long-term behavior is set by the sign of $\operatorname{Re}(\lambda)$ --- a single positive eigenvalue makes the whole system unstable.

quiz.prompt: What trial solution turns $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ into an eigenvalue problem?
Substituting $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ and cancelling $e^{\lambda t}$ gives what?
Why do eigenvectors simplify $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$?
quiz.answer: Along an eigenvector the system decouples into a scalar equation $x'=\lambda x$.

quiz.prompt: How is the general solution built from eigensolutions?
quiz.answer: As a linear combination of the eigensolutions $e^{\lambda_i t}\mathbf{v}_i$.

quiz.prompt: General solution for eigenpairs $(\lambda_1,\mathbf{v}_1),(\lambda_2,\mathbf{v}_2)$?
Solution for eigenvalue $\lambda=-2$ with eigenvector $\mathbf{v}$?
For real $\lambda$, what does the sign say as $t\to\infty$?
quiz.answer: Negative $\lambda$ gives decay toward the origin; positive $\lambda$ gives growth.

quiz.prompt: What behavior do complex eigenvalues produce?
quiz.answer: Oscillation, since complex eigenvalues produce sines and cosines.

practice.subtitle: Practice Set: Problems and Complete Solutions
practice.intro: Work the following ten problems in order. They climb through linear combinations and bases, the matrix of a transformation, the determinant and its geometric meaning, the similarity transform, the characteristic equation and the trace/determinant shortcut, eigenvectors, the eigen-ansatz, and a capstone that solves a coupled system $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ end to end.
problems: \textbf{(Basis test)} State the two conditions a set must meet to be a basis, then decide whether $(1,0)$ and $(2,0)$ form a basis of $\mathbb{R}^2$.
\textbf{(Matrix of a transformation)} For the matrix $A$ with rows $(1,2)$ and $(3,4)$, compute the image $A\mathbf{x}$ of $\mathbf{x}=(1,1)$.
\textbf{(Determinant, compute)} Find the determinant of the matrix with rows $(3,1)$ and $(2,4)$, and state the factor by which it scales area.
\textbf{(Determinant, meaning)} Compute the determinant of the matrix with rows $(1,2)$ and $(2,4)$. Is the matrix invertible? What does this say about its columns?
\textbf{(Change of basis)} Write the formula that expresses a matrix $A$ in the basis given by a change-of-basis matrix $P$, and state what makes the result diagonal.
\textbf{(Characteristic equation)} Write the $2\times 2$ characteristic equation in terms of the trace and determinant, then find the eigenvalues of the matrix with rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$.
\textbf{(Eigenvectors)} For that same matrix (rows $(2,1)$, $(1,2)$), find an eigenvector for each eigenvalue.
\textbf{(Eigen-ansatz)} Show that substituting $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ into $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ produces the eigenvalue equation $A\mathbf{v}=\lambda\mathbf{v}$.

\textbf{(Capstone --- solve a system)} Using the eigenpairs from Problems 7--8, write the general solution of $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ for $A$ with rows $(2,1)$ and $(1,2)$, and classify the equilibrium at the origin.
solutions:
\noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quad Scale each vector and add componentwise:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{2(1,0) + 3(0,1) = (2,0) + (0,3) = (2,3).} \]
The scalars stay attached to their own directions; they do not merge into a single axis.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 2.}\quad A basis must be \emph{linearly independent} and must \emph{span} the space. The vectors $(1,0)$ and $(2,0)$ are parallel, since $(2,0)=2(1,0)$, so they are dependent and span only the $x$-axis:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\operatorname{span}\{(1,0),(2,0)\} = \{(t,0)\} \neq \mathbb{R}^2.} \]
They are \textbf{not} a basis of $\mathbb{R}^2$.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quad Dot each row with the input $\mathbf{x}=(1,1)$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{A\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1+2 \\ 3+4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 7 \end{pmatrix}.} \]

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quad Use $\det = ad-bc$ with $a=3,b=1,c=2,d=4$:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det A = (3)(4) - (1)(2) = 12 - 2 = 10.} \]
The map scales every area by a factor of $\lvert\det A\rvert = 10$, and since $\det A>0$ it preserves orientation.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quad Compute the determinant:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\det\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix} = (1)(4) - (2)(2) = 4 - 4 = 0.} \]
A zero determinant means the matrix is \textbf{not invertible}: it collapses the plane onto a line, so its columns are linearly dependent (indeed $(2,4)=2(1,2)$).

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quad The similarity transform rewrites $A$ in the new coordinates:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{B = P^{-1} A P,} \]
where the columns of $P$ are the new basis vectors in the old coordinates. If those columns are eigenvectors of $A$, then $B$ is \textbf{diagonal}, with the eigenvalues on the diagonal.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quad The characteristic equation is
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda^2 - (\operatorname{tr}A)\lambda + \det A = 0.} \]
For rows $(2,1),(1,2)$: $\operatorname{tr}A=4$, $\det A=3$, so
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3 = (\lambda-3)(\lambda-1) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \lambda = 3,\ 1.} \]

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quad Differentiate the trial $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ (with $\mathbf{v}$ constant) and substitute:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}' = \lambda e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}, \qquad A\mathbf{x} = e^{\lambda t} A\mathbf{v}.} \]
Setting $\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}$ and cancelling the nonzero scalar $e^{\lambda t}$ leaves
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\lambda\mathbf{v} = A\mathbf{v},} \]
exactly the eigenvalue problem.

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quad Each eigenpair contributes one exponential mode; superpose them:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\mathbf{x}(t) = c_1 e^{3t}\begin{pmatrix}1\\1\end{pmatrix} + c_2 e^{t}\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix}.} \]
Both eigenvalues are real and positive, so every trajectory grows without bound: the origin is an \textbf{unstable node} (a source).

takeaway.title: Unit Key Takeaway
takeaway.body: Every problem above is one geometric story: a matrix \emph{moves} space. Its \textcolor{accentorange}{columns} say where the basis lands, its \textcolor{accentorange}{determinant} says how area is scaled and whether orientation flips, and its \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenvectors} mark the directions it merely stretches. Feeding those eigenpairs into $\mathbf{x}=e^{\lambda t}\mathbf{v}$ turns a coupled system into independent exponential modes --- the exact machinery Unit 15 runs on.
